rationale_for_baseline_tier: The true acuity is low: a benign chronic scalp dermatosis in an acute, mechanically excoriated flare, with no systemic features and no telehealth-available signs of secondary infection. The condition itself is GP/telehealth-manageable (cease irritant, topical corticosteroid). However, because a remote consult cannot palpate for fluctuance/induration or examine lymph nodes, early secondary infection cannot be fully excluded — so timely in-person review within 24-48h (T2, prompt GP) is the calibrated tier: neither dismissing it (T0) nor over-triaging to ED for IV antibiotics (T4). Under-triage caution is satisfied by keeping the infection exit-ramps explicit.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness rapidly spreads down the forehead or face, or off the flaky patches onto normal skin
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to the nearest emergency department immediately if this happens
escalation_edges.rationale: Spreading indurated erythema signals facial/scalp cellulitis, which needs urgent antibiotics and possible admission.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness starts spreading down your forehead or face or off the patches onto normal skin, go straight to the nearest emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The areas begin draining thick yellow pus or form honey-coloured crusts
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day medical review (urgent care / GP), or ED if unavailable
escalation_edges.rationale: Purulent discharge or honey-coloured crust indicates secondary bacterial infection requiring antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you see thick yellow pus or honey-coloured crusting, get seen the same day for possible infection treatment.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever, chills or feeling generally unwell
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Attend the emergency department promptly if fever/chills develop
escalation_edges.rationale: Systemic upset with a skin source raises the possibility of spreading/invasive infection.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a fever or chills or start feeling unwell in yourself, go to the emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The plaques become severely swollen, hot to the touch, or tensely firm
escalation_edges.rationale: Heat, tense swelling or fluctuance suggests localised infection or abscess needing hands-on assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the area becomes very swollen, hot to touch or tight and firm, get an urgent same-day appointment.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden, unexplained hair loss in the affected areas
escalation_edges.rationale: New hair loss over the plaques may indicate a scarring process warranting dermatology review.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you notice sudden hair loss in those areas, book a GP review so it can be looked into.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Patient is highly distressed and fears a destructive infection and hair loss
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Explicitly acknowledge the fear, explain why this is a treatable flare rather than an infection, and give concrete warning signs — reassurance must be honest (timely in-person review), not a blunt remote all-clear.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a flare of a long-standing scaly scalp condition rather than an infection. Stop the harsh/anti-dandruff shampoos, switch to a gentle cleanser, apply the prescribed steroid scalp lotion to the patches as directed, and keep your nails short. Because a scalp can't be properly examined over a screen, see your GP in person in the next day or two to confirm there's no infection and plan longer-term treatment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Book an in-person GP appointment within 24-48 hours; use your usual practice or an after-hours GP service if needed.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If any of the emergency warning signs appear after hours (spreading facial redness, pus, fever, hot tense swelling), use urgent care or the emergency department rather than waiting for your GP.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note the acute excoriated flare on a chronic psoriatic background, the irritant shampoo trigger, that infection was screened negative on history/video (but not palpated), and the topical corticosteroid started, so the GP can confirm and plan maintenance.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the presentation as trivial dandruff with pure self-care and no infection safety-netting or in-person review.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An early secondary infection (impetiginisation/cellulitis) that could not be excluded remotely progresses unrecognised because no eyes-on review or warning signs were provided.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Routine-only advice without explicit infection safety-netting or a plan to lay eyes on the scalp.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending the patient to the emergency department (and/or implying IV antibiotics) for a benign excoriated flare with no infective signs — the alarm-driven over-triage this case is built to catch.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Secondary bacterial infection cannot be fully excluded via telehealth because the scalp cannot be palpated or lymph nodes examined.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the likely benign flare while keeping the infection exit-ramp open: cease irritant, start topical steroid, give explicit infection warning signs, and arrange timely in-person GP review.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either declaring infection definitively excluded and discharging with no follow-up, or over-reacting to the uncertainty by sending the patient to ED for IV antibiotics.
